items.q: If 6 workers take 8 hours to paint a wall, how long would 12 workers take (assuming they work at the same rate)?
items.correct: 4 hours — this is inverse proportion: double the workers, halve the time.
items.misconception: 16 hours, because doubling the workers doubles everything including the time.
items.random_wrong: 8 hours, because each worker still has to do the same job.
items.why: Students default to direct proportion (more of one = more of the other) and don't recognise contexts where more of one means less of the other.

items.correct: -9 — without brackets, the exponent applies only to the 3, then the negative is applied: -(3^2) = -9.
items.misconception: 9, because squaring a negative number always gives a positive.
items.why: Students don't distinguish -3^2 from (-3)^2; the first is -9 because squaring binds tighter than the unary minus, but the second is +9.

items.correct: No — sin is not a linear function; sin(2x) = 2 sin(x) cos(x).
items.misconception: Sin is a function, so sin(2x) = 2 sin(x) by pulling the constant out.
items.random_wrong: Sin(2x) equals sin(x)^2 by analogy with squaring.
items.why: Students treat sin like a multiplier ('sin times x') rather than a function — so they 'distribute' or factor it incorrectly.

items.q: A force of 10 N acts perpendicular to a rod, 0.5 m from the pivot. Moment is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 5 Nm. “20 Nm” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: For a body in equilibrium, the sum of moments about any point is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: zero. “positive” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Find the moment of a 20 N force acting perpendicular to a lever, 2 m from the pivot.
items.why: Exact answer: 40 Nm. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
